ACADEMIC ANALYTICS
MIT Collaboration Network — Chord & Matrix
— — Hover arcs or ribbons for details

Legend

How to read this

Arcs (the colored segments around the ring) represent schools or colleges. Arc size is proportional to that school’s total collaborative works.

Ribbons (the curved shapes connecting arcs) show cross-school co-authorship. Ribbon width = number of shared works between those two schools. Within-school collaboration is not shown — only cross-school ties appear.

Hover any arc or ribbon to see the exact work count. Click an arc to isolate that school’s connections.

Why does Engineering look tangled? Engineering is MIT’s largest school by faculty and publication volume, so it has ribbons connecting it to almost every other school. The density reflects real breadth of collaboration, not a display error.

Note on joint appointments: Faculty with appointments in more than one department are counted in each of those departments. Their works are therefore attributed to all departments they hold simultaneously — so totals across arcs can exceed the count of distinct publications. This is by design: it reflects how interdisciplinary faculty genuinely contribute to multiple units.

MIT Collaboration — Global Matrix (Option B)

Each cell = distinct works linking any author in the row unit with any author in the column unit. Diagonal = works with ≥2 authors in the same unit (portal “Within” numbers).
⚠ Option B overview: cells are not additive — a joint-appointment author counts toward every unit they belong to. Do not sum rows or columns; use the Counts table for anchored totals.
Works (sqrt scale):    diagonal = within-unit
